title: Asking someone out
setting: You've been chatting with someone you like at a friend's gathering. There's a lull. You want to ask them out.

steps.speaker: Du
steps.es: (You want to suggest meeting again. How do you open?)
steps.en: (You want to suggest meeting again. How do you open?)

options.es: Es isch luschtig gsii mit dir. Wämmer öis mal uf e Kafi träffe?
options.en: It's been fun with you. Shall we meet for a coffee sometime?
options.feedback: Warm, low-pressure, and clear. A coffee invite is the perfect first step.

options.en: Will you give me your number?
options.feedback: Direct, but a reason to meet lands softer than just asking for digits.
Too much, too soon — it can feel intense before you've even met up.

steps.speaker: Sie
steps.es: Ja, sehr gärn! Wänn häsch Ziit?
steps.en: Yes, gladly! When do you have time?

options.en: Does Saturday afternoon work for you?
options.feedback: You offer a concrete option — much easier to say yes to than 'whenever'.
A bit vague — proposing a specific time shows you mean it.
Leaves it hanging after she said yes — seize the moment with a real plan.

steps.speaker: Sie
steps.es: Samschtig passt mer guet. Wo treffemer öis?
steps.en: Saturday works for me. Where shall we meet?

options.es: Es git es schöns Kafi am Schwanenplatz. Um zwöi?
options.en: There's a nice café at Schwanenplatz. At two?
options.feedback: Place and time sorted in one line. Smooth and confident.
Flexible is friendly, but having a suggestion ready feels more sure of yourself.
Pushing the decision onto her reads as uninterested. Offer a spot.

steps.speaker: Sie
steps.es: Super, denn bis Samschtig! Ich freu mi.
steps.en: Great, see you Saturday then! I'm looking forward to it.

options.feedback: Easy, warm, and mirrors her energy. Perfect sign-off.
A touch flat after she said she's excited — match her warmth a little.
Reads as distrustful and kills the mood. Keep it light and positive.
